explicit_text: I was activated for Operation Desert Shield in early December 1990 while I was in the Army Reserve. My unit was based in Wood River, Illinois, and shortly after activation, we went to Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri for training. Then we deployed to Saudi Arabia where we waited for our equipment before moving closer to the Iraq-Kuwait border. It was a tense time as we prepared for what was to come, and I was a small arms repairman supporting the troops.

explicit_text: Operation Desert Storm was the ground war that followed Desert Shield, and I was right there driving fuel trucks into Iraq. We moved up from Saudi Arabia into Iraq, supporting the fighting units near Kuwait's airport. The ground war lasted only a few days, but it was intense, and I remember the road we took back and forth, which the media called 'The Highway to Hell' because of all the destruction. It was a surreal experience, especially as a 19-year-old.

explicit_text: I joined the United States Army Reserve in December 1988 through a program that allowed me to do basic training between my junior and senior years of high school. I served as a small arms repairman with a unit based in Wood River, Illinois. During the Gulf War buildup, my reserve unit was activated for Desert Shield and Desert Storm, and I deployed with them to the Gulf. I left the Reserves in February 1992 as an E-3 before joining the Navy.

explicit_text: After my time in the Army Reserve, I joined the United States Navy in February 1992. I went through Navy basic training at Great Lakes, Illinois, and advanced training in Meridian, Mississippi. I then served in Puerto Rico for about fifteen years until my medical retirement in 2007 as an E-6. My Navy career included deployments during Operation Enduring Freedom and Operation Iraqi Freedom.

explicit_text: I received the Army Commendation Medal for volunteering to drive fuel trucks during the ground war in Desert Storm. General Norman Schwarzkopf Jr. personally presented the medal to me and the other volunteers in Saudi Arabia. He called us some of the craziest individuals he'd ever heard of because we took on such a risky job just to have something to do.

I met him when he personally awarded me and seven others the Army Commendation Medal for volunteering to drive fuel trucks. Despite his rank, he came across as a down-to-earth, grandfatherly figure, which made the experience surreal and memorable.
